Title: Turn a Half-Baked Idea Into a One-Page PRD
Teaser: Converts a rough feature idea into a crisp, build-ready product requirements doc with problem, scope, and success metrics on a single page.
PromptHint: Describe the feature or product idea in [IDEA]. Mention the user and the problem if you can; the rest can be vague.
Prompt:
You are a senior product manager who writes PRDs that engineers and designers can build from without a kickoff meeting. Here is the raw idea:
<idea>
[IDEA]
</idea>
Target user or context (optional): [USER]
Think first, silently: separate the real problem from the proposed solution, and flag where my idea is actually an assumption that needs validating.
Then write a one-page PRD with these sections: (1) Problem statement in one or two sentences, from the user's point of view. (2) Why now / why this matters. (3) Goals and explicit non-goals (what we are deliberately NOT building). (4) Target user and the single core use case. (5) Requirements as a numbered list of user stories in 'As a [user], I want [action] so that [outcome]' form, each tagged Must / Should / Could. (6) Success metrics: 1-3 measurable signals that would tell us this worked. (7) Open questions and key assumptions to validate.
CONSTRAINTS: No technical implementation details or tech-stack choices. No invented market statistics. Keep it to one page. If a section is unknowable from my input, write 'NEEDS INPUT:' and the specific question.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled sections above as Markdown headings, requirements as a numbered list, metrics as a short table (Metric | Target | How measured).

Title: Build a Quarterly Roadmap From a Pile of Requests
Teaser: Takes a messy list of feature requests and goals and shapes them into a themed, sequenced quarterly roadmap with rationale you can defend.
PromptHint: Paste your backlog, requests, or goals into [ITEMS]. Add the quarter's main objective in [OBJECTIVE] if you have one.
Prompt:
You are a head of product building a roadmap that tells a clear story, not just a list of features. Here is everything on the table:
<items>
[ITEMS]
</items>
This quarter's primary objective (optional): [OBJECTIVE]
Time horizon: [HORIZON]
Think first, silently: group items into 3-5 strategic themes, and notice which requests are symptoms of the same underlying need.
Then build the roadmap: (1) Name 3-5 themes, each with a one-line 'why this theme now'. (2) Under each theme, place the relevant items as outcomes (what changes for the user), not just feature names. (3) Sequence the themes across the horizon (e.g., Now / Next / Later) with a sentence of reasoning for the order. (4) Call out dependencies and the single biggest risk to the plan. (5) List what you are explicitly deferring and why, so stakeholders see it was a choice.
CONSTRAINTS: Tie every theme back to the objective if one is given. Do not promise specific calendar dates unless I supplied them. No filler items I didn't mention.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A Now/Next/Later table (Theme | Outcome | Items | Rationale), then a short 'Dependencies & risks' list, then a 'Deliberately deferred' list.

Title: Plan a Sprint That Actually Fits the Team's Capacity
Teaser: Turns a backlog plus your team's real availability into a committable sprint plan with a clear goal, sized work, and a buffer for the unexpected.
PromptHint: List candidate backlog items in [BACKLOG]. State team size and any time off or known interruptions in [CAPACITY].
Prompt:
You are an experienced delivery lead running sprint planning for a small team. Candidate backlog for the sprint:
<backlog>
[BACKLOG]
</backlog>
Team capacity (people, days, known time off / on-call / meetings): [CAPACITY]
Sprint length: [LENGTH]
Think first, silently: estimate relative effort for each item (T-shirt sizes S/M/L if no points given), and reserve realistic buffer for support, reviews, and unknowns.
Then produce the plan: (1) Propose a single sprint goal in one sentence that the committed work ladders up to. (2) Recommend which items to commit vs. defer, given the capacity, and show the rough effort total against available capacity. (3) For each committed item, note its definition of done in one line and any blocker or dependency. (4) Flag the top 2 risks to finishing the sprint and a mitigation for each. (5) Suggest a small 'stretch' list to pull from only if ahead.
CONSTRAINTS: Do not overcommit; leave 15-20% buffer. If capacity is too small for the goal, say so plainly and recommend what to cut. No invented velocity numbers.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Sprint goal' line, a Committed table (Item | Size | DoD | Dependency), an effort-vs-capacity summary line, a 'Top risks' list, and a 'Stretch' list.

Title: Prioritise Your Backlog With a Transparent RICE Score
Teaser: Scores and ranks competing initiatives using the RICE framework so your priority order is defensible, not just a gut call.
PromptHint: List the initiatives or features to compare in [ITEMS]. Add any data you have (reach, effort) so estimates are grounded.
Prompt:
You are a product operations lead who makes prioritisation decisions auditable. Items to rank:
<items>
[ITEMS]
</items>
Known data or constraints (audience size, effort estimates, strategic goal): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: for each item estimate Reach (how many users per time period), Impact (use a 0.25 / 0.5 / 1 / 2 / 3 scale), Confidence (a percentage), and Effort (person-weeks). Where you must guess, mark it.
Then score: (1) Compute RICE = (Reach x Impact x Confidence) / Effort for every item. (2) Rank items high to low by score. (3) For each item, give a one-line justification for the inputs and clearly tag any value you estimated vs. one I provided. (4) Surface the 'sleeper' (low effort, surprisingly high score) and the 'trap' (high excitement, weak score). (5) Note which estimates most affect the ranking, so I know where to gather data before committing.
CONSTRAINTS: Show the actual numbers, not just labels. Never present a guessed input as fact. No invented user counts beyond what context implies.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ranked table (Rank | Item | Reach | Impact | Confidence | Effort | RICE | Note on inputs), then a short 'Sleeper / Trap / Validate first' summary.

Title: Write a Status Report Executives Will Actually Read
Teaser: Compresses messy project updates into a tight, honest status report with a clear RAG signal, what changed, and what you need.
PromptHint: Dump your raw notes, updates, and blockers into [UPDATES]. Name who the report is for in [AUDIENCE].
Prompt:
You are a program manager known for status reports leaders trust because they hide nothing. Raw material from the week:
<updates>
[UPDATES]
</updates>
Audience (e.g., exec team, client, sponsor): [AUDIENCE]
Project and reporting period: [PROJECT]
Think first, silently: decide the single overall status (Green / Amber / Red) and the one thing this audience most needs to know.
Then write the report: (1) A one-line headline plus an overall RAG status with a half-sentence reason. (2) 'Since last update' — 3-5 bullets of concrete progress, outcomes not activity. (3) 'Risks & blockers' — each with owner and the help needed. (4) 'Decisions or approvals needed from you' — make the ask explicit and time-bound. (5) 'Next period focus' — the 2-3 things that matter. Keep the whole thing skimmable in 60 seconds.
CONSTRAINTS: Be honest about slippage; do not bury Red status in optimistic language. No jargon the audience wouldn't use. Don't invent progress that isn't in my notes.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Headline + RAG badge line, then four labeled bullet sections. Keep under 200 words unless I say otherwise.

Title: Build and Score a Living Project Risk Log
Teaser: Turns vague worries about a project into a structured risk register with likelihood, impact, scoring, owners, and concrete mitigations.
PromptHint: Describe the project and anything that could go wrong in [PROJECT]. List known concerns; the model will surface hidden ones too.
Prompt:
You are a risk manager who turns anxiety into an actionable register. Project and known concerns:
<project>
[PROJECT]
</project>
Key constraints (deadline, budget, team, dependencies): [CONSTRAINTS]
Think first, silently: brainstorm risks across categories — scope, schedule, resourcing, technical, external/vendor, stakeholder, and adoption — including ones I did not mention.
Then build the log: (1) List 8-12 distinct risks. (2) For each: rate Likelihood (1-5) and Impact (1-5), compute a score (L x I), and assign a severity band (Low / Medium / High / Critical). (3) Give each a specific, owned mitigation (reduce likelihood) and a contingency (what we do if it happens anyway). (4) Suggest an owner role and an early-warning trigger to watch. (5) Highlight the top 3 risks to manage actively this week.
CONSTRAINTS: Mitigations must be concrete actions, not 'monitor closely'. Don't pad the list with duplicates. No invented numbers about my specific project.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A sorted-by-score risk table (Risk | Category | L | I | Score | Severity | Mitigation | Contingency | Owner | Trigger), then a 'Top 3 to act on now' list.

Title: Draft a Stakeholder Update Tailored to Each Audience
Teaser: Takes one project update and reshapes it into the right message for execs, your team, and external partners — same truth, different altitude.
PromptHint: Paste the core update in [UPDATE]. List the audiences you need to reach in [AUDIENCES].
Prompt:
You are a communications-savvy project lead who knows the same news needs different framing for different rooms. Core update:
<update>
[UPDATE]
</update>
Audiences to address (e.g., executives, project team, external client, finance): [AUDIENCES]
Desired tone / channel (optional): [TONE]
Think first, silently: for each audience, identify what they care about (outcomes vs. tasks vs. money vs. timeline) and what they need to do with the information.
Then write one tailored update per audience: (1) Open with the point THAT audience cares about most. (2) Include only the detail relevant to them — execs get outcomes and asks, the team gets specifics and next steps, clients get impact and reassurance. (3) End each with a clear, audience-appropriate call to action or 'no action needed'. (4) Keep the underlying facts identical across versions — change emphasis, never the truth.
CONSTRAINTS: No spin that contradicts the core update. Don't make one version admit a problem and another hide it. Match the channel's length (a Slack note is shorter than an email).
OUTPUT FORMAT: One labeled block per audience, each with a subject/opening line and a short body. Note the suggested channel for each.

Title: Generate a Go/No-Go Launch Checklist
Teaser: Produces a thorough, owner-assigned launch checklist across product, ops, support, and comms so nothing gets forgotten before you ship.
PromptHint: Describe what you're launching in [LAUNCH]. Add the date and team in [DETAILS] so timing and owners make sense.
Prompt:
You are a launch manager who has shipped enough times to know what teams forget. What's launching:
<launch>
[LAUNCH]
</launch>
Launch date, team, and channels (optional): [DETAILS]
Think first, silently: map the launch across functions — product readiness, QA, infrastructure/ops, support enablement, marketing/comms, legal/compliance if relevant, analytics/tracking, and rollback.
Then build the checklist: (1) Group tasks under those functional headings. (2) Make each item a verifiable yes/no check ('Rollback plan documented and tested', not 'think about rollback'). (3) Tag each with a suggested owner role and a timing relative to launch (T-7, T-1, launch day, post-launch). (4) Define the explicit Go/No-Go criteria — the conditions that MUST be true to ship. (5) Add a short post-launch monitoring list for the first 48 hours.
CONSTRAINTS: Include analytics/tracking and rollback even if I didn't mention them. If money, health, or legal exposure is involved, add a check to get the right professional sign-off (not professional advice). No filler.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Checkboxes grouped by function (Owner + Timing on each line), a bold 'GO/NO-GO CRITERIA' block, then a 'First 48 hours' monitoring list.

Title: Run a Blameless Retrospective From Your Raw Notes
Teaser: Synthesises messy retro input into honest themes, root causes, and a few owned, dated action items instead of a venting session.
PromptHint: Paste what went well, what didn't, and any frustrations into [RETRO NOTES]. Name the project or sprint in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are an agile coach facilitating a blameless retrospective focused on systems, not scapegoats. Raw input from the team:
<notes>
[RETRO NOTES]
</notes>
Project or sprint context: [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: cluster the raw comments into themes, and for each negative theme push past the symptom toward a likely root cause.
Then facilitate on paper: (1) 'What worked' — 3-5 themes worth keeping, phrased so the team can repeat them. (2) 'What hurt' — themes with a brief, blameless root-cause note (process/communication/tooling, never a named person). (3) 'Aha / learnings' the team should carry forward. (4) Action items: a SHORT list (max 5) of specific changes, each with a suggested owner role and a 'by when'. Prefer fewer real changes over a long wish list. (5) One experiment to try next time and how we'd know it helped.
CONSTRAINTS: Stay blameless — describe behaviours and systems, not individuals. Don't generate 12 vague actions; force prioritisation. Use only what's in the notes plus reasonable inference.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Four labeled sections (Worked / Hurt+Root cause / Learnings / Actions table: Action | Owner | By when), then a one-line 'Experiment to try'.

Title: Write a Crisp Project Charter to Kick Off Right
Teaser: Turns a fuzzy new project into a one-page charter — objective, scope, stakeholders, milestones, and success criteria — so everyone starts aligned.
PromptHint: Describe the project, its purpose, and who's involved in [PROJECT]. Add the deadline or budget in [CONSTRAINTS] if known.
Prompt:
You are a PMO lead who prevents scope chaos by nailing the charter before any work starts. The project:
<project>
[PROJECT]
</project>
Known constraints (deadline, budget, sponsor, team): [CONSTRAINTS]
Think first, silently: distinguish the business objective from the deliverables, and pin down what's in scope vs. explicitly out, because that's where projects go wrong.
Then write the charter: (1) Purpose & business objective — why this project exists, in two sentences. (2) Scope — bulleted in-scope items and a clear 'OUT OF SCOPE' list. (3) Key deliverables and 3-5 milestones with rough sequencing. (4) Stakeholders & roles — sponsor, lead, and who is Responsible/Accountable/Consulted/Informed at a high level. (5) Success criteria — how we'll know it's done and worked. (6) Assumptions and the top constraints/risks. (7) Open questions to resolve before kickoff.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep it to one page. Be explicit about out-of-scope items even if obvious. Don't invent budgets, names, or dates I didn't give — mark them 'TBD: confirm with sponsor'.
OUTPUT FORMAT: The seven labeled sections as Markdown, scope and out-of-scope as bullets, milestones and RACI as small tables.

Title: Reverse-Engineer a Timeline From a Hard Deadline
Teaser: Works backward from a fixed launch date to a realistic, buffered schedule with milestones, dependencies, and the moment you'd have to descope.
PromptHint: List the major deliverables or phases in [WORK]. State the fixed end date and start date in [DATES].
Prompt:
You are a delivery planner who builds backwards-pass schedules and is honest when a date is unrealistic. Work to be done:
<work>
[WORK]
</work>
Fixed deadline and available start date: [DATES]
Team size / known constraints (optional): [CONSTRAINTS]
Think first, silently: identify dependencies (what must finish before what), then schedule backward from the deadline, inserting buffer before the hard date.
Then build the plan: (1) Break the work into phases/milestones in dependency order. (2) Assign each a rough duration and the latest start/finish to still hit the deadline (backward pass). (3) Mark the critical path — the chain with no slack. (4) Insert an explicit buffer before launch and say how much. (5) Give a blunt feasibility verdict: comfortable / tight / unrealistic, and if tight or unrealistic, name exactly what to descope or parallelise to make it fit.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't pretend an impossible timeline works — flag it. Use relative durations if I gave no team size. Account for dependencies; don't schedule blocked work in parallel.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A milestone table (Milestone | Duration | Latest start | Latest finish | Depends on | On critical path?), then a 'Feasibility verdict' line and a 'If it's too tight, cut/parallelise these' list.

Title: Prep a Decision Brief That Gets a Yes (or a Clear No)
Teaser: Frames a stalled project decision into a tight brief with options, trade-offs, and a recommendation so the right person can decide fast.
PromptHint: Describe the decision you need made and the options in [DECISION]. Note who decides and any constraints in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a chief of staff who unblocks teams by making decisions easy to make. The decision on the table:
<decision>
[DECISION]
</decision>
Decision-maker, deadline, and constraints (budget, time, strategy): [CONTEXT]
Think first, silently: clarify the actual decision being asked (not the surrounding noise), and identify the 2-4 genuinely viable options including 'do nothing'.
Then write the brief: (1) The decision in one sentence, framed as a clear question. (2) Why it matters now and the cost of delay. (3) Options — for each: a one-line description, key pros, key cons/risks, and rough effort or cost. (4) A clear recommendation with the reasoning, and what you'd need to be wrong. (5) The specific ask: who decides what, by when, and what you need from them to proceed. Keep it to a single screen.
CONSTRAINTS: Always include a 'do nothing / status quo' option. Make a real recommendation — don't hedge into mush. If the decision involves money, legal, or health exposure, add a one-line 'confirm with a qualified professional' note (not professional advice). Use only facts I gave or reasonable inference.
OUTPUT FORMAT: 'Decision' line, a short 'Why now' note, an options comparison table (Option | Pros | Cons/Risks | Effort/Cost), a bold 'Recommendation' paragraph, and a 'The ask' line with owner + deadline.
